narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63.4% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like family income and study plans, comparing them to patterns seen in thousands of past students. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 class ranking, your Year 1 class ranking, and—countering that somewhat—your upper-middle family income bracket. Most heavily, your Year 3 decile of 1 stood out as a strong signal of struggle, far below the typical 6th decile among those who passed the bar, suggesting challenges in sustaining performance that the model associates with higher failure risk. Your Year 1 decile of 2 reinforced this concern, again well under the 6th decile average for successful bar takers, indicating early academic hurdles that often predict ongoing difficulties. On a brighter note, your upper-middle family income aligned well with the majority of passers—about 45% came from similar backgrounds—providing some push toward a passing prediction by matching a common profile for success. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 28.5 (below the 37.3 average for passers) and undergraduate GPA of 2.6 (versus 3.24 for those who succeeded), played supporting roles in the overall risk assessment, as did your full-time study plans, which are typical for admittees. Compared to accepted students, who generally showed stronger early and later rankings plus higher entrance metrics, your profile signaled elevated bar exam risk to the model. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—many with similar starts have turned things around—but the system prioritizes those with trajectories matching proven bar passers to protect program outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework if possible, and gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment. Building a clearer upward trend in rankings, perhaps via post-bac programs or targeted tutoring, could reshape your profile for future applications. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized feedback, and explore bridge programs designed for determined students like you aiming for law. With focused steps, you can align more closely with successful profiles and reapply stronger.
